page.title: Turning 65 & Medicare — Your Step-by-Step Guide | Vernal Medicare
page.heading: Turning 65: Your Medicare Step-by-Step
page.description: Turning 65? Here’s exactly what to do, in order — when to enroll, whether to delay for employer coverage, Medigap vs Advantage, Part D, costs, and IRMAA. Free local help in the Uintah Basin.
page.summary: Turning 65 is when Medicare starts — and the choices you make now are some of the hardest to undo. This is the whole journey in {stepCount} steps: when your window opens, whether to delay for employer coverage, how to sign up, the big Medigap-vs-Advantage decision, drug coverage, and what it all costs. Work through it here, or let us walk you through it free.

faqs.question: When should I sign up for Medicare if I’m turning 65?
faqs.answer: Your Initial Enrollment Period is the 7 months around your 65th birthday — 3 months before your birthday month, that month, and 3 months after. Sign up in the first 3 months for the earliest coverage start.

faqs.question: Do I have to enroll at 65 if I’m still working?
faqs.answer: Not always. If you have active, creditable employer coverage, you may delay Part B without penalty and get a Special Enrollment Period later. Retiree and COBRA coverage usually don’t count — check before you delay.

faqs.question: Medigap or Medicare Advantage — which should I pick at 65?
faqs.answer: There’s no single right answer. Medigap offers go-anywhere coverage and predictable costs with a separate Part D plan; Medicare Advantage bundles everything with a network and an out-of-pocket cap. It’s the most consequential (and hardest to reverse) choice — we compare both with you free.

nextSteps.action: call us free at {phone} — we’ll build your personal plan and timeline, no pressure.

body.introHtml: "I'm turning 65 — what do I actually do?" Here's the whole thing, in order. Each step links to a deeper guide or a calculator, and you can hand any of it to us to do together, free.
body.sixtySecondHtml: The 60-second version: your enrollment window opens 3 months before your birthday month. Enroll in Part A & B (unless you have creditable employer coverage). Then make the big choice — Medigap or Medicare Advantage — add Part D drug coverage, and check your costs and IRMAA. Not sure? Call {phone}.
everythingItemsHtml: Not sure this is your path? Start with your situation — we’ll route you to the right center.
Still working? Working past 65 — whether to take Medicare now or delay it.
